sections.heading: The national level edged down, but the component measures moved differently
sections.body: Nationally, 4.9 percent of teens had experienced pregnancy in 2025, down from 5.4 percent in 2022. Live birth also declined slightly, from 3.8 percent to 3.5 percent, and current pregnancy fell from 1.6 percent to 1.3 percent. Pregnancy loss moved in the other direction, from 0.4 percent to 0.5 percent, so the headline decline does not mean every component indicator moved down.

sections.heading: The highest regional levels are well above the national figure
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had the highest share of teens who experienced pregnancy in 2025 at 9.3 percent, almost double the national figure of 4.9 percent. MIMAROPA and SOCCSKSARGEN followed at 8.2 percent each, and both were also high on begun childbearing. The regional pattern is therefore not a narrow outlier story; several regions sit far above the national level.

sections.heading: Some regions rose sharply from 2022
sections.body: SOCCSKSARGEN increased from 3.8 percent of teens who experienced pregnancy in 2022 to 8.2 percent in 2025. MIMAROPA also rose substantially, from 5.0 percent to 8.2 percent. These increases matter because the national rate declined over the same period, which means the overall trend masks sharp regional movement in different directions.

sections.heading: Other high-rate regions moved down but remained elevated
sections.body: Northern Mindanao fell from 10.9 percent in 2022 to 6.4 percent in 2025, and Caraga fell from 7.7 percent to 6.5 percent. Both remained above the national figure in 2025 despite the decline. This makes the regional story a mix of persistent elevation and recent change, rather than a simple ranking of current levels.

sections.heading: Current pregnancy is not concentrated in exactly the same places as total experience
sections.body: Negros Island Region had the highest current pregnancy measure at 3.8 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 3.5 percent and MIMAROPA at 3.1 percent. These are not the same regions that simply top the overall experienced-pregnancy ranking. The contrast suggests that current pregnancy adds useful timing information beyond the cumulative measures of live birth and begun childbearing.

sections.heading: The age pattern is steepest at the oldest teen ages
sections.body: The share of teens who experienced pregnancy rose from 1.5 percent at age 15 to 9.7 percent at age 19 in 2025. The largest single-year jump in the 2025 age series was from age 18 to 19, where the rate increased by 2.8 points. Age 19 also moved down substantially from 13.3 percent in 2022, so the oldest teen group still has the highest rate but no longer reaches the 2022 level.

sections.heading: The rural-urban gap is present but not the main source of variation
sections.body: Rural teens had a higher experienced-pregnancy rate than urban teens in 2025, 5.8 percent compared with 4.2 percent. Both residence groups declined from 2022, with rural teens down from 6.1 percent and urban teens down from 4.8 percent. The 1.6-point residence gap is meaningful, but it is much smaller than the spread between the highest and lowest regions.

sections.heading: Education differences are sharpest at the lower grade levels
sections.body: Teens with Grades 1-6 education had the highest experienced-pregnancy rate in 2025 at 17.2 percent, with begun childbearing also high at 16.2 percent. Grades 7-10 and Grades 11-12 were much lower at 4.7 percent and 5.1 percent, while college was lower still at 2.6 percent. The no-education and post-secondary rows have very small denominators or suppressed values, so the clearest comparison is between Grades 1-6 and the larger education groups.

sections.heading: The wealth gradient is strong, but not every quintile moved the same way
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had the highest experienced-pregnancy rate in 2025 at 9.4 percent, compared with 1.4 percent in the highest quintile. The lowest, middle, and highest quintiles declined from 2022, while the second and fourth quintiles increased slightly. Pregnancy loss was also highest in the lowest quintile at 1.9 percent, adding another dimension to the wealth pattern.

takeaways: Teenage pregnancy declined nationally from 5.4 percent in 2022 to 4.9 percent in 2025.
Regional variation is wide, with Zamboanga Peninsula¹, MIMAROPA, and SOCCSKSARGEN far above the national level.
The highest current-pregnancy levels do not exactly match the highest cumulative teenage pregnancy levels.
Teenage pregnancy remains much higher among teens in the lowest wealth quintile and among those with Grades 1-6 education.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
